summary:
Empress Lü Zhi (241–180 BC) was the wife of Han dynasty founder Liu Bang (Emperor Gaozu) and the effective ruler of China during the reigns of Emperors Hui and Shao (195–180 BC), making her one of the most powerful women in Chinese imperial history. After her husband's death, she dominated the court through her position as empress dowager, executing rivals, placing her Lü clan relatives in key military and administrative positions, and sidelining the Liu imperial family.

Her regency period was politically turbulent but administratively capable — she continued the early Han policy of light taxation and minimal government interference (Huang-Lao Daoism) that allowed the empire to recover from the civil wars. Her notorious cruelty toward her personal rivals, particularly her treatment of Concubine Qi (whom she had mutilated and displayed as the 'human pig'), became the defining image of her reign in later Chinese historiography. After her death in 180 BC, the Liu family generals launched a coup, exterminated the Lü clan, and placed Emperor Wen on the throne — firmly establishing the principle that the imperial family alone should hold power.

figures.name: Empress Lü
figures.summary: Empress of Han China from 202 to 195 BC, regent from 195 to 180 BC
